data.title: 7 Stocks I'm Buying As The U.S. Economy Starts To Crack
data.description:
I once heard a preacher say:

A passion to teach without a burden to study is a desire to perform.

Those words stuck with me.

We financial writers are teachers, of sorts. Let me be quick to clarify that this doesn't mean we are always right or are smarter than anyone else. Intelligent comments on my articles frequently demonstrate that. It is humbling to remember that the collective knowledge of my readership is vastly superior to my own.

In any case, when your line of work involves putting out content for general audience consumption, there is very often the temptation to perform.

How else is one supposed to be noticed in a world awash in all sorts of competing content?

At some point, about a year ago if memory serves, I decided to focus on quality over quantity in my public writing and analysis.

I write fewer articles today than I have in many years, but each one is the product of many long hours of study, reading, and research. My hope is to deliver as many useful insights into the economy, markets, and investing as I can each week.

This week's article is no exception.

On that note, let's hop to it.

Here's the agenda:

  • A follow-up on DOGE and staying politically neutral in a polarized age.
  • Would Medicaid cuts negatively impact skilled nursing facility REITs?
  • A deep dive on single-family rental REITs.
  • Some troubling signs that a negative feedback loop may be forming in the economy.
  • An update on market rotation in 2025.
  • Some thoughts on new additions to my 7-stock buy list.

Onward.

On DOGE And Neutrality In The Age of Polarization

A quick word on political neutrality in economic and investment analysis.

In last week's article, "8 Stocks I'm Buying Amid A Market Rotation Toward Dividend Stocks," I included a section on DOGE, making the argument that it will not be able to sustainably cut its initially promised $2 trillion, nor its pared down target of $1 trillion, nor even its most recently pared down target of $500 billion.

If it was able to do that, there would be significant implications for the economy and markets.

But, I concluded, "Rather than a Javier Milei-esque shift toward austerity and efficiency, investors would do well to expect more of the same from Washington DC."

In the pinned comment, I made this clarification:

I am happy to see DOGE finding and rooting out some government waste. But without enlisting or even consulting experts in government spending (such as the GAO, the CBO, or the nonpartisan Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget), there are bound to be many mistakes, and much of what they cut will be added back by Congress later. To monitor what is actually going to happen with fiscal policy over the long term, watch what Congress does, not what DOGE does.

Because of my statements on this subject, several commenters accused me of being politically biased.

While I don't claim to be unbiased (everyone's got a bias), I do claim to be nonpartisan in my economic and investing analysis. I think it's vitally important for investors not to mix political loyalties with their thinking on matters related to investments or the economy.

Let me give a few examples.

First, surveys show that partisans on both sides of the aisle experience wild swings in their expectations for GDP growth, stock market performance, and inflation based on which party wins elections. After the 2024 election, Republicans' inflation expectations plunged, while Democrats' soared.

I tend to think that the independents' response is the only one worth paying attention to.

Second, consider energy policy and the investment implications thereof.

The fossil fuels industry was elated when President Trump won the 2016 election. But after an initial bounce in energy stocks (XLE), the sector proceeded to severely underperform the rest of Trump's first term, even before COVID-19 began.

Despite Trump's visceral disdain for renewables, especially "windmills," renewable energy producers (RNRG) performed quite well during his first term, massively outperforming energy stocks even before the pandemic began.

Other forces in the economy proved more powerful than the bully pulpit.

Conversely, President Biden (and especially some of the staffers in his administration) viscerally loathed oil & gas and did more than any other president in history to advance renewables, and yet renewable power producers massively underperformed oil & gas stocks during his term.

Again, other forces in the economy proved more powerful than the bully pulpit.

Back to DOGE.

Over the past week, I believe the views I expressed in last week's article have basically become consensus.

Polymarket, the same betting market that hugely favored Trump to win in 2024, has a way for users to bet on how much spending DOGE will cut this year. While the betting odds initially favored "over $250 billion," they have since come to heavily favor "less than $50 billion."

Perhaps this is why, until recently, Wall Street economists have not weighed in much on DOGE.

That changed when Apollo Global's chief economist, Torsten Slok, argued that DOGE's ~300,000 federal layoffs (plus potentially hundreds of thousands of government contractors) creates significant near-term headwinds for the US economy and could be weighing on business and consumer sentiment.

Ultimately, though, other fiscal factors will prove more powerful than a unilateral bureaucratic purge by the executive branch.

One such factor (rather obviously) is Congress.

James A. Baker III and John W. Diamond recently wrote in the Wall Street Journal:

The nondefense discretionary spending that DOGE has focused on is only 16% of the federal budget.

... America can’t achieve a sustainable budget without reforming Social Security, Medicare and other healthcare benefits. Together, these accounted for 49% of federal spending last year, and the share is growing over time.

If Congress and the President came together to pass a comprehensive plan to cut across-the-board spending, including entitlements, this would certainly have market-moving implications.

But that isn't what's happening.

Very recently, President Trump stated in an interview that mandatory items like Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid wouldn't be touched. His words:

But, look, Social Security won’t be touched... Medicare, Medicaid, none of that stuff is going to be touched.

That immediately removes half the federal budget from potential cuts. (Over half, if you include interest expense.)

The budget resolution framework that passed in the House on Tuesday calls for $4-4.5 trillion in tax cuts (positive for markets) but only $1-2 trillion in spending cuts. The nonpartisan Committee For A Responsible Federal Budget estimates that this framework would add $2.8 trillion to deficit spending (negative for markets).

Cutting government spending sufficient to balance the budget (or at least get close) would ultimately benefit the US economy by lowering interest rates as well as bolstering Uncle Sam's creditworthiness and the Dollar's global supremacy.

The Italian economist Alberto Alesina and his colleagues wrote a whole book on this topic called Austerity: When It Works And When It Doesn't.

But in my estimation, the haphazard, chaotic, and uncoordinated way that DOGE is going about it will ultimately prove self-defeating and will have little long-term impact on interest rates or markets.

It is yet another opportunity for long-term budgetary reform in DC that appears to be passing by.

Andrew Sullivan puts it bluntly:

Imagine what they might have done. Trump could have announced that Musk and his minions were going in to audit the federal government. Within a few months, they’d bring a report, outlining every insane piece of waste or DEI excess or fraud they could find. Trump would then urge Congress to vote on these reforms. Win, win, win. It’s a great idea to shake up the joint with an outsider! But nah. They are busy ensuring that any cuts they make are brutal, dumb, and destined to expire.

That's harsh, and not how I would phrase it, but the bottom line is that the ultimate fiscal destiny of the country rests on Congress, not DOGE.

Investors would do well, in my judgement, to temper their expectations of federal spending cuts.

Potential Medicaid Cuts' Effect On Healthcare REITs

Competing bills and amendments in Congress are proposing various ways to modify and reduce Medicaid funding.

These funding cut proposals seem to have spooked the skilled nursing REIT space, as SNF operators derive a huge portion of total revenue from Medicaid.

While most healthcare REITs are flat to up this year, the SNF REITs like Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI), CareTrust REIT (CTRE), and Sabra Health Care (SBRA) are down between 3-5% year-to-date:

Paradoxically, SNF REITs have bounced a bit since the House passed a budget framework that would order various committees to find $880 billion in cuts (over 10 years), much of which would have to come from Medicaid.

If all of that came from Medicaid, it would represent about a 10% cut of total Medicaid spending.

But only about 1/3rd of total Medicaid spending goes to long-term care, including SNFs.

Keep in mind President Trump's recent statement that Medicaid wouldn't be touched and that Senate Republicans intend to repeal the unrealistically high staffing requirement for nursing homes that has been a burden to many operators. Moreover, by all accounts, meaningfully cutting Medicaid spending is an uphill battle that invites a wide array of pushback.

Moreover, even within the House of Representatives, there are at least 10 congressional Republicans who have indicated hesitation about Medicaid cuts.

It's useful to remember that the resolution that passed was for a framework of tax and spending cuts, and the Senate will undoubtedly alter it to some degree.

Here's CTRE's CEO Dave Sedgwick from the Q4 2024 earnings conference call:

Of course, there's some noise and speculation about what the new administration means for skilled nursing. It's too early to be definitive, but our conversation with policymakers, lobbyists, operators, all leads us to believe that the minimum staffing rule will be reversed and that Medicaid and Medicare will continue to be unchanged as the cornerstones of healthcare in general and skilled nursing in particular.

... There's just no room really to cut Medicaid for skilled nursing. And I think the states and the federal government are more aware of that today than they ever have been based on what happened with the pandemic.

OHI's SVP of Operations, Megan Krull, similarly expressed confidence on the Q4 conference call that if any Medicaid cuts are passed, they are most likely to pertain to Medicaid expansion that covers non-elderly adults.

SBRA's CEO Rick Matros concurred on his company's Q4 earnings call:

As it pertains specifically to Medicaid cuts, Congress has been historically protective of the elderly population, particularly those vulnerable institutionalized folks. The Medicaid budget, inclusive of matching funds is critical to the governors of all states, both red and blue. And in fact, the red states have been the greater recipients of Medicare, of Medicaid access, the expansion of Medicaid access in recent years.

... The House budget has $880 million of unspecified Medicaid -- $880 billion of unspecified Medicaid cuts. The Senate version has no Medicaid cuts and overturns the staffing mandate.

While anything could happen, I view the current selloff as a good opportunity to buy more of my favorite SNF REIT -- CareTrust.

What's Going On With Single-Family Rental REITs?

The gap between the affordability of renting and buying a home is huge right now, and Redfin forecasts that this affordability gap will widen further in 2025 as home prices and mortgage rates both remain high.

Globe Street notes that this setup may provide a further benefit to big SFR investors this year, as developers may be more willing to cut deals to sell homes to investors (including SFR REITs) if they have trouble selling them to owner-occupiers.

So why have SFR REITs been underperforming the broader real estate index (VNQ) year-to-date as well as the last 6 months?

One reason is simply the fact that a huge amount of housing, both single-family and multifamily, was delivered last year. It will take time to absorb that amount of housing, and in the process, rent growth for single-family and especially multifamily (especially in certain Sunbelt markets) should remain muted.

Another factor is that tariffs, especially on Canadian lumber, could significantly increase the cost of construction, which is especially relevant for AMH since it has an internal development program that churns out ~2,000 homes per year.

Yet another potential factor that could affect homebuilders, including AMH, is deportations, since a significant portion of residential construction workers are unauthorized immigrants. So far, though, deportations do not seem to have meaningfully impacted homebuilders' operations.

And then there's potential regulatory issues down the road.

At the tail end of Biden's presidency, in January 2025, Lina Khan's Federal Trade Commission initiated a study on "mega" SFR investors that own over 1,000 homes, seeking to understand how these institutional investors have impacted home prices and rent rates.

The assertion is that mega SFR investors drive up home prices and rent rates.

Some evidence suggests that the impact of major SFR investors is somewhat more benign.

For example, a 2023 study by Joshua Coven of NYU Stern School of Business found that while major SFR investors have increased home prices by 7.4 percentage points per 1 percentage point of ownership in a given market, they have also greatly increased the supply of high-quality rental homes on the market. As a result, SFR rent rates have declined by 2.3 points for every 1 point of major SFR investor ownership in a given market, making quality rental homes more affordable and accessible to renters.

To quote the study's abstract:

The findings suggest that institutional investors made it harder for people to purchase homes, but easier for renters to access neighborhoods that previously had few rentals.

Major SFR landlords are less a pure negative than a tradeoff.

Moreover, using data from the 10-Ks of Invitation Homes (INVH) and American Homes 4 Rent (AMH), the Urban Institute notes that SFR REITs typically spend between $15,000 and $40,000 on upgrades to homes acquired from the existing home market, whereas the typical owner-occupier spends only $6,300 on upgrades.

Their extensive experience, internal capacity, reliable vendor relationships, and discounts yield more cost-effective renovation than a homeowner could generally achieve for the same work.

This could be because SFR REITs are more likely to buy "fixer-uppers," or maybe owner-occupiers are simply more willing to live with outdated or lower-quality amenities.

In either case, the result is a higher quality housing stock.

Lastly, as someone who used to work at a residential management company, I would assert that another underappreciated benefit of highly scaled SFR ownership is professional management.

Big SFR landlords like INVH, AMH, and Tricon Residential (acquired by Blackstone) have the scale to manage a large portfolio of rental homes in their markets with speed and efficiency. This is a benefit both to the landlord and the tenant.

Compare this to small landlords, who generally lack scale and efficiency, as well as third-party management companies that generally aren't incentivized to manage and maintain properties as much as the owners themselves.

If and when it comes time for SFR "mega-landlords" to defend their place in the housing market before legislators, I think they'll have a decent case to make.

Ultimately, my assumption is that AMH and INVH are languishing right now because of the market's belief that the US housing market will weaken and home prices will decline. The soaring rate of home delistings certainly lends credence to this theory.

So does the level of unsold new homes sitting on the market:

If home prices drop, then the net asset values ("NAVs") of the SFR REITs should likewise go down.

That's the only way I can make sense of the fact that AMH trades at a ~15% discount to NAV while INVH trades at a ~20% discount.

The worst-case scenario for SFR REITs is that home prices drop significantly at the same time as mortgage rates decline, causing SFR REITs' NAVs to drop even as demand for their product declines as people choose to buy instead of rent.

Troubling Signs In The Economy

While the economy is by no means bad right now, there are some cracks that seem to be forming.

As macroeconomist Neil Dutta recently wrote in Business Insider:

Household consumption and government investment accounted for the lion's share of growth last year, while gross private domestic investment — which tracks companies putting money into their businesses and the amount spent on building new homes, apartments, and other structures — was a modest drag. Given the narrow composition of growth, it stands to reason that if consumer spending and government investment moderate, the economy will, too. The case for a slowdown in both categories is strong.

Between hundreds of thousands of federal government layoffs and fresh promises of new tariffs coming from the White House, the two engines of growth Dutta mentions (consumer and government spending) could sputter out this year.

There was a lot of hubbub about the drop in consumer confidence recorded in surveys by The Conference Board and the University of Michigan. The main focus in the media on these surveys was the spike in inflation expectations.

While the egg shortage and related hikes in egg prices certainly don't help, it appears the main reason why consumers' inflation expectations are rising is the slow realization that Trump is actually serious about imposing tariffs (as I have been saying for a long time).

Recently, on CNBC, former Trump administration official Marc Short stressed that Trump is indeed very serious about his protectionist policies.

While they may be useful for negotiations with foreign countries, they are not mere negotiating tactics. President Trump has long held the view that trade imbalances are unfair and advocated tariffs as a remedy.

The missing piece in this anti-trade logic is that foreigners have to do something with the US dollars received for their goods. That is usually either to buy US exports or invest in US assets (Treasuries, stocks, real estate, etc.).

The unintended effect of increasing tariffs is to reduce the flow of USD to foreigners, which, all else being equal, raises the value of the USD and thereby makes US exports more expensive.

It should also, all else being equal, decrease the flow of foreign investment into the US.

But perhaps the scarier part about the recent consumer confidence reports is the expectations for the labor market.

In the Conference Board survey, the share of consumers who expect fewer jobs in 6 months soared.

Now, to be fair, this is just a survey. It's soft data, not hard data.

But there are other indications that the labor market is on the verge of weakening. With home starts stagnating, it appears that there will soon be far too many residential construction workers employed than there is available work -- about 750,000 jobs' worth, according to one model from 3Fourteen Research.

If consumers are onto something -- if the labor market does meaningfully weaken in the coming months -- there could be a negative feedback loop forming in the economy.

  • Fewer people working >>> less wage growth >>> sluggish or falling consumer spending >>> pullbacks in business investment >>> even weaker labor market >>> Etc.

There are no red flags going up as of yet, and maybe there won't be any. Maybe we'll continue to muddle through, as we have the last few years. But economic risks are more elevated today than they've been in a while.

Market Rotation Monitor

As I began to discuss last week, the market appears to be in the midst of a rotation from growth to value that is favorable to dividend stocks such as those in the iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV), Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM), and Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

Surprisingly, not even Nvidia's (NVDA) impressive Q4 2024 earnings beat and strong guidance have been able to halt its and the Magnificent 7's (MAGS) selloff over the last few weeks.

My impression is that, rather than pertaining to any earnings or guidance specifics, the market is simply shifting into risk-off mode.

My Defensive Buy List

The market going into risk-off mode also explains why long-term bond yields have dropped somewhat year-to-date, which in turn explains why my largely defensive, REIT-heavy portfolio is outperforming.

Here's how my five largest holdings are performing against the S&P 500 (SPY) so far this year:

The last few weeks have been particularly kind to defensive dividend stocks.

Zooming out further to the last year, I am a bit surprised to find that all but casino REIT VICI Properties (VICI) have outperformed the S&P 500:

Having high-quality and defensive stalwarts like Agree Realty (ADC), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) as portfolio anchors does cause some drag when the stock market is rip-roaring in risk-on mode.

But when the market gets defensive, these names really shine.

Steady, reliable compounders. That's the name of the game for me.

And these are the kinds of stocks on my buy list right now as well.

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.3% High-Single-Digit
Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) 5.5% Mid-Single-Digit
CareTrust REIT (CTRE) 4.5% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
Healthpeak Properties (DOC) 6.0% Low-Single-Digit
Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) 0.5% Double-Digit
Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI) 5.9% Mid-Single-Digit
Permian Resources (PR) 4.2% Mid-Single-Digit
Vanguard Ultra-Short Term Bond ETF (VUSB) 4.7% N/A

I took American Tower (AMT) off my buy list after its post-earnings stock price pop to >$200. It's bittersweet, because I wasn't done building my position.

The market seemed to particularly like AMT's ~$600 million planned capex budget for US data centers via its CoreSite subsidiary.

I'm happy to see this as well, but the dividend growth outlook is unchanged -- mid-single-digits. Ideally, I'd get a little higher starting yield than AMT's current 3.2% to pair with that dividend growth rate.

Maybe I'll get a pullback.

Canadian Natural Resources

CNQ is Canada's largest oil producer and has a leading market position in Canada's oil sands fields. The company has deleveraged considerably in recent years while also repurchasing shares and hiking its dividend. That dividend should remain covered unless the price of oil falls below $40.

The biggest reason for CNQ's recent selloff is the trade spat between the US and Canada, with President Trump threatening tariffs on all Canadian imports. But my understanding is that Canadian oil imports will only be taxed at 10%, rather than the 25% rate for everything else. (Of course, the specifics could change by the time this article is published.)

I believe the negative impact to CNQ from these tariffs will be less than what is currently priced in.

And ultimately, the mutual benefit that Canada and the US enjoy from thier close trading relationship make a sustained trade conflict unlikely.

Alphabet

I don't yet own GOOGL in my taxable account, although I do own some in my Roth IRA. The stock's ~10% selloff YTD looks like a great buying opportunity.

The breadth and enduring competitive advantages of GOOGL's various assets, from Search to Gemini to Cloud to Gmail to Waymo to YouTube, is truly mind-blowing.

Consider this: YouTube alone almost generates as much revenue as Netflix (NFLX).

And while the market is nervous about GOOGL's $75 billion capex budget for this year, I do believe it is necessary to keep the AI innovation moving forward.

As explained by Christopher Mims in the Wall Street Journal, most of the Big Tech capex spending this year is going toward more computationally intensive reasoning models rather than the (now conventional) large language models or chatbots like DeepSeek.

At the time of writing, GOOGL trades at about a 19x P/E multiple. That strikes me as remarkably reasonable for such a high-quality and innovative tech conglomerate.

Of course, its dividend yield of 0.5% is minimal, but so is its ~10% payout ratio. At some point, perhaps when AI capex cools down, I would expect dividend growth to kick into high gear.

Permian Resources

PR is a smaller oil producer and a pure-play on the Permian basin. It enjoys among the lowest costs per barrel of any producer in the Permian and Delaware basins. It is also absolutely gushing free cash flow right now, with which it has been paying down debt and working toward an investment grade credit rating.

At a price of $14.15, PR sports a massive free cash flow yield of 11.6%, based on 2024's actual results.

As long as the price of oil doesn't collapse, PR should continue to generate ample cash flow to continue bolstering its balance sheet and boosting its dividend.

Vanguard Ultra-Short Term Bond ETF

Last Fall, I raised some cash and then deployed it into a few oversold names in the immediate aftermath of the election. I'm starting to feel inclined toward raising cash again. This will be difficult because I have so many attractive stocks on my buy list, but I am going to try to accumulate some cash in VUSB.

VUSB is a similar cash-like vehicle as the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury ETF (SGOV), but VUSB goes a bit further out in duration and takes slightly more risk with investment grade corporate debt and commercial paper. Because of this, VUSB's yield of 4.7% is a bit higher than SGOV's current 4.2%.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: How To Find The Best Style Mutual Funds Q1 2025
data.description:
With an ever-growing list of similar-sounding mutual funds to choose from, finding the best is an increasingly difficult task. How can investors change the game to shift the odds in their favor?

Don’t Trust Style Mutual Fund Labels

There are at least 836 different All Cap Value mutual funds and at least 5,348 mutual funds across twelve styles. Do investors need 445+ choices on average per style? How different can the mutual funds be?

Those 836 All Cap Value mutual funds are very different. With anywhere from 14 to 955 holdings, many of these All Cap Value mutual funds have drastically different portfolios with differing risk profiles and performance outlooks.

The same is true for the mutual funds in any other style, as each offers a very different mix of good and bad stocks. All Cap Value ranks first for stock selection. Small Cap Growth ranks last.

Avoiding Analysis Paralysis

We think the large number of style mutual funds hurts investors more than it helps. Manually conducting a deep analysis for every fund is simply not a realistic option, exposing investors to insufficient analysis and missing profitable opportunities. Analyzing mutual funds, with the proper diligence, is far more difficult than analyzing stocks because it means analyzing all the stocks within each mutual fund. As stated above, there can be as many as 955 stocks or more for one mutual fund.

Figure 1 shows our top-rated mutual fund for each style.

Figure 1: The Best Mutual Fund in Each Style

* Best mutual funds exclude funds with TNAs less than $100 million for inadequate liquidity

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Amongst the mutual funds in Figure 1, Royce Small-Cap Special Equity Fund (RSEIX) ranks first overall, Fidelity SAI U.S. Value Index Fund (FSWCX) ranks second, and FullerThaler Behavioral Small-Cap Equity Fund (FTHFX) ranks third. Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Fund (VMGRX) ranks last.

How to Avoid “The Danger Within”

Why do you need to know the holdings of mutual funds before you buy?

You need to be sure you do not buy a fund that might blow up. Buying a fund without analyzing its holdings is like buying a stock without analyzing its business and finances. No matter how cheap, if it holds bad stocks, the mutual fund’s performance will be bad.

PERFORMANCE OF FUND’S HOLDINGS – FEES = PERFORMANCE OF FUND

If Only Investors Could Find Style Mutual Funds Rated by Their Holdings

Royce Small-Cap Special Equity Fund (RSEIX) is not only the top-rated Small-Cap Value mutual fund, but is also the overall top-ranked style mutual fund out of the 5,348 style mutual funds that we cover.

The worst mutual fund in Figure 1 is Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Fund (VMGRX), which gets an Attractive rating.

This article was originally published on January 28, 2025.

Disclosure: David Trainer, Kyle Guske II, and Hakan Salt receive no compensation to write about any specific stock, sector or theme.

data.title: Alpha Metallurgical Resources: Adding More To This Coal Giant
data.description:
Brief Thesis Walkthrough

Investing in coal might seem like a risky idea now given the large global push for renewables which will phase out energy generation methods like burning coal. When in 2021, Alpha Metallurgical Resources (AMR) began generating a profit after a few shaky years the market reacted very positively. The question is now whether or not foreign markets which make up a clear majority of AMR revenues can support rising prices over the long term.

The short answer is yes. India is responsible for 13% of all export revenues and will continue to import larger quantities of met coal in 2025 and beyond. Investors have to look past pessimism for the industry and realize there are bargains to be had still. I have had a position in AMR since 2022 and will be adding more this week.

AMR Market Position And Current Market Climate

AMR is a mining company located in North America. It works with the exploration, excavation, and processing of thermal coal and met coal, the latter also known as coking coal. In the US, AMR owns assets in Virginia And West Virginia. The US has, over the past decade, made a significant effort to move away from coal and into renewable energy instead. This means that AMR doesn't actually generate a majority of its revenue from domestic sales. According to a presentation for Q4 of 2024, AMR exported 76% of all its production. In its 10-K, AMR described how India is the largest customer. AMR has two countries which is responsible for over 10% of all revenues each, India and Brazil. Over the past 5 years, 37% of all exported sales for AMR have been in India.

Because of this revenue mix, AMR is heavily impacted by the growth of developing countries like India. Developing countries are the ones that haven't moved in the same direction as the US in terms of generating energy. This makes sense as it requires immense amounts of capital and right now it's cheaper to buy coal instead to generate energy. In the case of India, the imported met coal is used to fire up steel production. Globally India is expected to have one of the fastest growing steel markets, recording a 4.1% CAGR from 2024 through to 2050. At the same time globally the CAGR would be 1%.

Some might describe AMR as a leading US coal producer, but I would say this description is wrong. According to EIA in 2023, AMR was the tenth-largest coal producer. The ranking accounts for both thermal and met coal production. According to the report, the largest producer was Peabody Energy Corporation (BTU), representing 18% of all production, compared to AMRs 2.8%. The interesting thing is that, based on market cap, AMR is actually the larger company, $2.4 billion compared to $2.08 billion. However, FWD estimates for earnings have AMR as the cheaper option. 5.46x earnings in 2026 compared to 6.28x earnings for BTU.

According to AMR, the company has 316 million tons of coal reserves still across its 4 major production areas and 21 mines:

Mid-West Virginia Underground - 127 million tonnes

Mid-West Virginia Surface - 62 million tonnes

West Virginia Surface - 58 million tonnes

Virginia - 69 million tonnes

The numbers are from 2023, which is the latest AMR that has been released. Accounting for an annual production of 17 million tonnes, we can estimate roughly 298 - 299 million tonnes remain or 17 years' worth of production.

Market Climate And Pricing Differences

During Q4 AMR generated $493 million in met coal revenues and $25.6 million from thermal coals. Because of this large discrepancy, I will be primarily looking at met coal onwards. Globally pricing for met coal differs significantly depending on the market. By far AMR can realize solid pricing domestically, averaging $158.9/tonne in Q4. The Australian Indexed pricing was $124.7/tonne and the “other pricing mechanisms” was $122.2/tonne, representing 46% of all met tons sold.

On average AMR netted $127.8/tonne sold. BTU has already released its Q4 report. From the BTU report, it's clear that it realized lower prices QoQ, much like AMR now did. BTU reported exported prices fell from $105.51/tonne to $96.41/tonne, down 9.4%. The decline was lower for AMR pointing to more strength in their contracted deliveries of coal and which markets they engage in. Ultimately, this means AMR is the superior coal investment right now.

In the 2025 guidance, AMR has 95% of all thermal exports already committed at $80.7/tonne but only 32% of all met coal exports at $143.8/tonne. This leaves AMR exposed to price fluctuations over the next 12 months.

It seems unlikely we ever see a spike like in 2022 for the price of coal. There was significant global uncertainty during this period of time. It was a perfect storm of increasing demand and falling production levels. The falling prices were followed by weaker demand as global fiscal policies were tightened and steel production fell. China was also contributing to the fall of the price as it continued to increase coal production and because of the subsidies that companies often received there, they could afford to dump supply on the global market which led to prices falling further. According to Enerdata Asia accounted for 74% of all global coal production in 2023.

Pricing Outlooks For Met Coal

In a report from World Bank Blogs, they noted that global met coal prices are likely to continue falling in 2025 and 2026 but remain above pre-pandemic levels. The reason for this is robust global production levels helping satisfy demand. By these estimates, I think a met coal price range could be $100 - $140 between 2025 and 2026. Given that AMR has a higher average price realization I think they will average on the higher end of this range. These outlooks mean that AMR can continue to aggressively buy back shares and deliver shareholder value this way rather than a dividend. It's more flexible for the company as it's at the mercy of commodity prices.

Management Comments And Outlook

"We reduced our metallurgical shipment guidance for the year by 500,000 tons, bringing the range to 14.5 million tons to 15.5 million tons. This is largely due to the impact we're already seeing in the first quarter of the severe weather impacts on our own production levels as well as the production of our peers, which we expect will lower the volumes of coal we purchased in 2025."

In the Q4 Earnings Call CEO Andy Eidson provided shipment guidance for 2025. 14.5 - 15.5 million tons of coal would equate to $1.6 - 1.7 billion in revenue at an average price realization of $116.83 per tonne. Should AMR maintain the same EBITDA margin as in 2024 it would generate roughly $340 million based on the revenue forecast.

"Due to the continued softness in the met coal markets, we did not repurchase any shares in the fourth quarter under the company's share buyback program."

CFO Todd Munsey followed up by commenting on AMR buybacks. Since a large portion of the shareholder returns will come from buybacks, a soft met coal market will have a detrimental effect on this. However, AMR maintains a 12% FCF margin which netted $723 million TTM. Out of this $253 million was used for buybacks. Based on the revenue forecasts for 2025 AMR would comfortably be able to dedicate $76 million to buybacks. This represents 3.17% of all outstanding shares. This is a conservative estimate and AMR has the financial position to dedicate more capital to its share buyback program. By 2023 the total buyback program reached a value of $1.5 billion. I fully expect AMR to continue this trend.

Renewable Investments Aren’t Coming Fast Enough

The most common argument against coal investments is that they will eventually be completely phased out. Well, over the long term absolutely. In the short to medium term it is highly unlikely it will have a material impact on the use of coal globally.

In 2023 EIA anticipated a slowdown in renewable investments at a time when it is the most needed perhaps to reach emission targets. Tighter monetary policies made investments less attractive as they would be more expensive and yield lower returns.

Between 2030 - and 2040 usage of coal will begin to slow down more quickly as countries such as China and India shift over to renewables at a faster pace. In the decade the decline annually would be 3%. This will hit AMR but even with 3% lower exports each year AMR would still generate sufficient FCF to fund a large buyback program.

High Short Interest And Depressed Stock Price

Over the past 3 years the share price of AMR has appreciated by 89.94%, but peaking much higher during a 2022 rally as the price of coal also rallied. Over the past 12 months, shares are down 51.47%. Short interest has also increased and is now at 7.58%. The relatively high short interest has been a key contributing factor as to why the share price has moved the way it has the past 12 months. Short sellers are pushing down the price of AMR as they don't believe in the long-term outlook of the coal industry. That's the way I would summarize it.

However, as I’ve covered, there are clear opportunities still present, primarily in developing economies. India will be a key customer over the coming decades. Through the revenues AMR generates here, it can divert this to shareholder returns. These returns, along with a reversal in AMR stock price once the valuation simply gets too ridiculously low will yield long-term investors with a higher return than what an index fund could return.

In comparison to Range Global Coal ETF (COAL) shares of AMR have underperformed by a wide margin. COAL is down 22.3% in the past 12 months. COAL has a 2.09% yield currently. If AMR utilizes the $400 million remaining capital in its share buyback program it would yield an immediate return of 16.6%. I fully anticipate these funds will be used within the next 24 months, netting 8.35% annually. This is a higher return than what I see COAL returning. Because of the market-specific exposure that AMR offers it can also deliver higher returns as opposed to COAL which “casts a wider net” so to speak.

Peer Group Comparison And Fair Value Assessment

I will be comparing AMR to another close peer, in this case, Warrior Met Coal (HCC). I’ve covered the stock before and came to the conclusion it was a buy. Since then, my recommendations have yielded a 3.05% return. Below are some key financial figures and metrics for both companies:

Market Cap

AMR - $1.93 billion

HCC - $2.61 billion

FWD Non-GAAP P/E

AMR - 10.4x

HCC - 12.6x

FWD EV/EBITDA

AMR - 3.6x

HCC - 6x

TTM Buybacks As A % Of Market Cap

AMR - 10.5%

HCC - 0%

TTM Levered FCF Margin

AMR - 12.17%

HCC - (10.18%)

I believe that these figures show that AMR is also a very solid coal investment right now. It inches slightly above HCC in terms of FWD EV/EBITDA, leaving a bigger potential upside. It's only slightly but coupled with the large buybacks that AMR engages in it concludes to potentially superior returns over the next 12 months.

On Seeking Alpha, the analysts are all very bullish whilst the quant rating hints at a sell instead. I lean more bullish, but I wouldn't make AMR a strong buy simply from the fact that the long-term outlook is under a lot of pressure. Because of this fact, I can’t make AMR the most bullish recommendation. However, I do realize the potential it can yield in the medium term.

A fair value for AMR would be an EV/EBITDA of 5x in my opinion. This multiple would be conservative and reflect the long-term risks facing the company. Compared to AMRs current multiple of 3.6x it leaves an upside of 38.8%.

Risks To The Thesis

Given that AMR is a commodity-driven business, its revenues for a given quarter of the fiscal year are completely up to what the price of said commodity is during the period. This means AMR is exposed to a lot of potential fluctuations between quarters if there is a lot of market uncertainty about oversupply or undersupply.

Over the long term, the risk is that coal would be phased out at a faster pace than what is estimated right now. This would potentially reduce the actual shareholder returns that AMR would be able to offer. Lower returns would be reflected by the share price sinking further.

Conclusion

AMR could quickly be disregarded as a legacy investment that has had its run already. I would say this is the wrong way to look at AMR. It still has the potential to return large sums of capital to shareholders just from the fact there is still a large global demand for met coal, especially in developing economies. AMR has a footprint in these already and will therefore experience the immediate benefit. Global met coal prices are also expected to remain above pre-pandemic levels, which, in my opinion, means the stock price of AMR has reached a floor here. The upside is 38.8% based on my fair value of 5x EV/EBITDA.

data.title: 2 Very Overrated And 2 Very Underrated High-Yield Stocks
data.description:
As most of my regular followers know, I love investing in high-yield stocks because they are generally much easier to value. Unlike growth stocks, where you need to count on the company sustaining high-growth rates well into the future to deliver attractive returns, high-yield stocks simply need to sustain their payouts and potentially support that with low to mid-single-digit annualized earnings per share growth—depending on how high the yield is—to generate strong total returns.

I then overlay my opportunistic capital recycling strategy on top of this approach to generate very solid returns over the course of many years, which I have been able to achieve in my portfolio.

That being said, not every high-yield stock is a good buy, as valuation matters significantly, as does the underlying quality of the business. With that in mind, in this article, I am going to share two overrated high-yield stocks as well as two underrated high-yield stocks.

Overrated High-Yield Stock #1

The first overrated stock I want to talk about is a business development company ("BDC") called FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK). It does have some positive attributes going for it, including being managed by a leading alternative asset manager in KKR (KKR) and making progress in improving underwriting. It also pays out an attractive dividend yield. However, I believe that its recent strong total return performance has pushed its valuation to a point where it no longer adequately compensates investors for the risks associated with the fund.

These risks include one of the highest expense ratios in the BDC space, as well as one of the highest amounts of payment-in-kind ("PIK") income. This means that FSK's earnings quality is among the lowest in the BDC sector, since PIK income is not cash payments on debt but instead involves counterparties paying back FSK with even more debt rather than actual cash. This reduces the reliability of its income stream. Additionally, FSK’s leverage ratio is not particularly low, as it sits right in the middle of its target range, meaning it does not have much room for financial flexibility.

On the valuation front, FSK’s price-to-NAV ratio is close to one, with it currently trading at just a 2% discount to its net asset value. While a 2% discount may appear cheap compared to some peers like Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), Main Street Capital (MAIN), and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), these other BDCs have much stronger track records in terms of underwriting and portfolio quality.

As a result, I think that FSK is overrated right now, as it should trade at a larger discount—somewhere between 5% and 10% of NAV—based on its current quality and relative valuations within the sector. Instead, I believe there is another BDC that is trading at a similar discount to its NAV but is of much higher quality. I think this underrated BDC presents a much better opportunity, and I will discuss it later in this article.

Overrated High-Yield Stock #2

Another overrated dividend stock right now is Kinder Morgan (KMI). While KMI is certainly a high-quality energy infrastructure company with one of the most impressive natural gas portfolios in the U.S.—making up 65% of its business, alongside a quality refined products business (26% of its portfolio) and CO₂ assets (9%)—its valuation has gotten ahead of itself.

It also has a solid investment-grade balance sheet, holding a BBB credit rating from S&P, and its dividend is highly stable, backed by 95% take-or-pay, fee-based, or hedged cash flows. Additionally, its payout is well covered by distributable cash flow.

However, the biggest issue with KMI is that its dividend growth profile is simply not impressive. Analysts only forecast a 2.2% CAGR through 2027, and even its distributable cash flow per share is only expected to grow at a 4.9% CAGR over that period. These are underwhelming numbers, especially for a company that has been bid up by Mr. Market due to expected tailwinds from the AI boom.

While KMI’s valuation has cooled somewhat as investors realize the initial AI enthusiasm was overdone, it still only yields 4.5%. When you combine a 4.5% yield with a meager 2.2% dividend per share CAGR, the total return outlook is simply not compelling—especially when there are other midstream companies with much higher yields, stronger growth rates, and equally strong (if not better) asset portfolios and balance sheets.

As a result, I think KMI is overrated right now, and later in this article, I will discuss another midstream infrastructure company that I believe presents much better risk-reward potential.

Underrated High-Yield Stock #1

Now for the BDC that, I think, is underrated, and that is Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL). The reason I believe MSDL is underrated, especially relative to FSK, is that it trades at a similar discount to net asset value, currently at a 3% discount as of this writing. However, it offers materially better fundamentals.

First, MSDL has a significantly lower expense ratio than FSK, making it more cost-efficient. Additionally, it has one of the lowest PIK income levels in the BDC sector, at just about 2%, whereas FSK’s PIK income level is in the mid-teens. Second, MSDL’s leverage ratio remains low, currently at 1.04 times, which is at the low end of its target range of 1.0 to 1.25 times. In contrast, FSK’s leverage ratio is materially higher at 1.12 times, making MSDL the more conservatively positioned of the two. Additionally, MSDL has a very low non-accrual rate, which is also significantly lower than FSK’s, and nearly 97% of its holdings are in quality first-lien senior secured loans.

Furthermore, Morgan Stanley (MS)—MSDL’s external manager—owns about 11% of the equity, meaning that management is well-aligned with shareholders. When you combine all these factors, MSDL is one of the highest-quality BDCs in the market. In fact, it is very similar to Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) in terms of portfolio quality and structure, yet MSDL trades at a roughly 25% discount on a price-to-NAV basis relative to BXSL.

Therefore, I see significant valuation multiple upside potential for MSDL, whereas I see none for FSK. In fact, I would not be surprised if FSK’s valuation contracted further.

Underrated High-Yield Stock #2

Finally, a midstream infrastructure company that, I think, is underrated, especially relative to KMI, is Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). This is because it has a much higher distribution yield of 6.7% and, instead of trading at over a 11x EV-to-EBITDA like KMI does, it trades at a 9.9x EV-to-EBITDA.

On top of that, it has a much stronger expected distribution per unit CAGR outlook of 5% through 2027, which is much higher than KMI's. Additionally, its distributable cash flow per unit CAGR over that period is 6.2%, which also significantly exceeds KMI's.

So, on both yield and growth, it is much more attractive than KMI. Yet, it also has a stronger balance sheet, as per its sector-best A- credit rating, compared to KMI's BBB. It has a lower leverage ratio of about 3.1x, compared to about 3.9x for KMI.

Additionally, while it may not have quite the vaunted natural gas portfolio that KMI does, it still has meaningful exposure to natural gas and is well-diversified into petrochemicals, NGLs, and oil-related assets, while also having significant exposure to data center-driven demand. This should enable it to profit from the AI boom, just as KMI will.

As a result, overall, I think that EPD makes way more sense as an investment than KMI does from a total return, income, growth, and risk-avoidance perspective.

Investor Takeaway

As this article discusses, high-yield stocks are a great way to relatively conservatively compound wealth and passive income over the long term. However, investors still need to be careful not to overpay, as high-yield stocks can also become victims of market irrationality, just as high-flying growth stocks can.

As a result, we discussed two overrated high-yield stocks alongside two very underrated high-yield stocks. It is these underrated, high-quality, high-yield stocks that we are loading up on in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: A Buy Rating For Allison Transmission With Modest Growth Expected In 2025
data.description:
Investment thesis

The share price of Allison Transmission Holdings, Inc. (ALSN) took a hit when the firm released its 2025 guidance on February 11.

Yet, the 110-year-old company has a solid business and expects modest growth this year. Even Wall Street analysts expect EPS to increase 7.5%.

Despite the market’s bearishness, I rate Allison a Buy and expect the share price to rise over the course of the next 12 months.

About Allison

The company calls itself a “leading designer and manufacturer of propulsion solutions for commercial and defense vehicles and the largest global manufacturer of medium- and heavy-duty fully automatic transmissions.”

This table from its 10-K for 2024 shows its domestic and international markets, and the main products within those markets:

It has global reach, with markets in North America, Asia, Europe, South America, and Africa. In those regions, it has a network of some 1,600 independent distributors and dealers. Just over three-quarters of its revenue, 77%, came from North America last year.

Allison’s transmissions, or propulsion solutions, go into medium- and heavy-duty on-highway commercial vehicles, off-highway vehicles and equipment, and defense vehicles.

At the close on February 27, 2025, it traded at $100.46, and it had a market cap of $8.59 billion.

Competition and competitive advantages

The company has reported it competes with “numerous” manufacturers of propulsion solutions for commercial vehicles. In addition, OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers) are increasingly using in-house products rather than outsourcing. Regarding this vertical integration, the company feels it remains competitive because of its quality, reliability, and branding.

In the off-highway niche, it competes with Caterpillar Inc. (CAT), Twin Disc, Incorporated (TWIN), and Dana Incorporated (DAN).

The company has several competitive advantages:

  • It has been in the transmission business since 1915, giving it more than a century’s worth of experience.
  • In a related vein, it has a well-known brand name and an extensive network of distributors and dealers.
  • Patents, trademarks, and other intellectual property give it technological edges over its competitors.

It also claims advantages based on innovation and technological leadership, according to this graphic from its website:

Are these strengths enough to provide Allison with a competitive advantage in its industry? To assess, we will compare its net income margin and return on equity [ROE] with Dana (market cap $2.25 billion) and Twin Disc (market cap $122.08 million). First, net income margins:

Allison is clearly head and shoulders above those competitors, and has been for the past decade. Will it perform as well on ROE? Yes:

Based on these comparisons, I consider Allison to have at least a medium moat.

Allison’s latest earnings

Its Q4-2024 and full-year 2024 results were released on February 11, 2025. In the news release it highlighted two metrics:

  • Q4 net sales were a record at $796 million, and contributed to a full-year sales record of $3.2 billion.
  • Full-year diluted EPS also set a record, at $8.31, a y-o-y increase of 12%.

It offered this enhanced income statement in its Q4 earnings call presentation:

To put these results, for the quarter and the year, into context, the following chart shows revenue, net income, and basic EPS over the past decade:

The chart shows Allison stayed on the growth track that began in the second quarter of 2021. Further, it has grown quite steadily, with minor amounts of variation, and bottom-line results have kept pace with top-line results.

From an owner's perspective, this is good, predictable growth that allows shareholders to, as the saying goes, sleep well at night.

This stability contrasts with the share price, which has shown significant fluctuations. In particular, the price dropped by 12.46% on February 12, the day after the earnings were released. According to Seeking Alpha News Editor Amy Thielen's analysis, “soft guidance” for FY25 upstaged its bullish results for the fourth quarter. We will address this issue further in the Growth section.

On the Q4 earnings call, CFO Frederick Bohley told analysts the firm had paid down over $100 million of its existing term loan debt. That, like the buybacks, should also reduce expenses and push up EPS.

Dividends and stock repurchases

Allison’s board of directors signaled confidence in the future by increasing the dividend and authorizing an additional $1 billion in share buybacks. The latter boosts the fund to $5 billion in total.

This is significant for a couple of reasons. First, its market cap is only $8.59 billion, so both $1 billion and $5 billion in repurchases would put a serious dent in the number of shares outstanding. In turn, that would materially increase earnings per share.

Second, the stock price is currently low in relation to where it was before February 12. Repurchasing now while the price is depressed could be a wise “investment” and could also get the price moving upward again.

The Board increased the dividend by 8.0%, boosting the quarterly amount from $0.25 to $0.27. At the close on February 27, with the share price at $100.46, the yield was 1.08%. In dollar terms, that means $1.08 annually (4 x $0.27).

The payout ratio is 12.03%, leaving a lot of room for future increases. Allison has increased the dividend for five consecutive years, and the average annual growth rate has been 10.76%.

Like the buyback initiative, the increased dividend lets investors know the company expects to keep growing profitably.

Allison’s growth prospects

Where did that “soft guidance” come from? Have a look at this slide from the earnings call presentation:

Critics certainly could argue that only one in five cylinders will be firing this year. Defense may grow 18%, but it is a relatively minor component of consolidated net sales; $212 million out of a total of $3.225 billion. On the other hand, Global Off-Highway is expected to decline by 22%, but this market makes up just $105 million on that $3.225 billion consolidated net sales figure.

Despite these numbers, the Wall Street analysts see reasonable earnings growth this year, and more than double that in 2026. They do not expect the 2026 boost to carry over into 2027 and 2028:

Their revisions are less bullish; only one Up and six Down.

On the earnings call, CEO David Graziosi said he expected the defense end market to continue driving the business because the U.S. and other countries are currently in a defense upcycle. As part of that growth, the company recently received a U.S. Department of Defense contract to provide new and upgraded X1100 transmissions for the Abrams Main Battle Tank.

He also is optimistic about the potential of the wide-body mining dump truck initiative, which should generate $100 million of incremental annual revenue.

What’s more, the company has the financial resources for growth. In its 2025 guidance, the company expects to have $800-$860 million in net cash from operating activities and $635-$685 million in adjusted free cash flow. Capital expenditures are forecast to be $165-$175 million.

Valuation

For context, the following chart shows the Allison share price over the past decade:

A couple of notes on the chart: First, share prices began their steep climb in the third quarter of 2022. They peaked at $120.35 on January 23, 2025 and subsequently fell to $98.35 on February 24, before posting a couple of days’ worth of gains.

Even with the recent downturn, Allison’s total return has handily outperformed the S&P 500 since the end of January 2024:

Turning to valuation, because of the pullback over the past month, the Price/Earnings ratios are below those of the Industrials sector and its PEG ratios are close to them. This is an excerpt from Allison's valuation table at Seeking Alpha:

If we consider Allison a growth stock, then we will also want to pay attention to those PEG ratios. On a GAAP, TTM basis, it is fairly valued and on a Non-GAAP, FWD basis it is slightly overvalued.

Based on the solid revenue, net income, and earnings growth we’ve seen over the past several years, I believe Allison is a growth stock. I also consider it fairly valued.

For most of the past decade, the share price has generally tracked earnings, as shown in this 10-year chart of price, basic EPS, and normalized diluted EPS:

Because of this relationship, I’m going to set a one-year price target based on projected earnings (as shown in the Wall Street analysts’ table, a 7.50% increase for this year).

That’s well above the 3.97% implied by net income guidance of $760 million (at the midpoint). Note that net income and EPS gains may differ because of changes in the number of shares outstanding over the course of this year.

Nevertheless, I will add 7.50% to the February 27 closing price, mainly because the previous rate of growth was much higher. Between March 31, 2021 and December 31, 2024, net income had a CAGR of 27.8%.

Adding 7.50% to the February 27 closing price of $100.46 provides a one-year target of $108.00.

That’s close to the target set by the Wall Street analysts: $109.50, which represents an increase of 9.25%.

With my target and the company’s growth initiatives, I rate Allison a Buy. One other Seeking Alpha analyst has posted a rating in the past 90 days, also a Buy. The Quant system has a Hold, and the Wall Street analysts also have a collective Hold. The latter is based on two Strong Buys, one Buy, four Holds, and three Sells.

Allison’s risk factors

The company operates in a competitive industry, one that sees advances through technological innovation and research. Investors should consider the potential of disruptive change in the industry.

All forecasts and estimates must, of necessity, be uncertain. Investors should consider my forecasts, as well as those of other analysts and the company, just one minor element of their due diligence.

The market had what I consider to be an overreaction to the company’s guidance for 2025. Following the herd should never be an investment strategy, even though there may appear to be a consensus about a company’s future.

More than half of Allison’s net sales originate with North American On-Highway vehicles. If economic conditions weaken, trucking fleets may be downsized, leading to lower sales for the firm.

As a global company, it is exposed to worldwide political and economic conditions. It also has exposure to shifting currency exchange rates, which may grow or shrink its bottom-line results.

Conclusion

The 2025 guidance offered by Allison Transmission Holdings may not have satisfied the market, but I consider it a worthy Industrials growth stock. The guidance and Wall Street estimates do not measure up to the results the company has delivered in recent years, but there should be at least modest growth ahead.

Allison has been around for multiple generations, and with my Buy rating, I’m signaling that I expect it to be around for more.

data.title: Spirit AeroSystems Heavily Leans On Boeing And Airbus Support
data.description:
In December 2024, I covered Spirit AeroSystems (SPR) and attached a sell rating to the name. Interestingly, the stock gained nearly 5% since then while the broader market declined 3.3%. In this report, I will discuss the most recent developments.

Spirit AeroSystems Shareholders Approve Acquisition by Boeing

Probably the most important event is that shareholders of Spirit AeroSystems voted in favor of the takeover by Boeing (BA) on the 31st of January. The votes were overwhelmingly in favor of the deal. To me, that is no surprise as Spirit AeroSystems really didn’t have another way out. It has a significant pile of debt and leaned on advances from Boeing and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF).

The transaction is expected to be completed in mid-2025. What might be somewhat surprising is that seemingly the exchange ratios or Boeing stock prices have not been adjusted for the capital raise that happened at Boeing. However, the capital raise at Boeing does not alter the transaction value of the deal and therefore it should also not be adjusted for the capital raise. So, shareholders of Spirit will hold a smaller portion of Boeing, but the transaction value remains the same. At the same time, it does mean that the risk of dilution at Boeing was transferred to shareholders.

Spirit AeroSystems Provides Preliminary Results

What is somewhat interesting is that the operating and free cash flow were positive during the quarter. The advances received are treated as a financing cash flow. So, the OCF and FCF reflect the operational performance which was strong year-on-year.

From an operational perspective, the year actually was not that bad for Spirit AeroSystems. The Boeing 737 program is obviously key to driving revenues and profits, but total shipset deliveries among all programs increased from 1,418 to 1,432. It is not the growth that was hoped for at the start of the year, but it does show that there is some recovery. The fourth quarter deliveries increased from around 400 in Q4 2023 to 457 in Q4 2024, which also shows there is recovery including a 30% increase in Boeing 737 shipset deliveries.

The company also shared that it had received $270 million in funding from Boeing and Airbus out of a total of $457 million. Consequently, half of the cash pile came from advances and 17% came from the positive free cash flow.

Nonetheless, the revenues fell short by around $800 million for the last three quarters of 2024 against the base plan. In fact, the results fell below the downside plan as well. This is driven by the work stoppage at Boeing which also resulted in supplier shipments being reduced. Analysts estimate $7.90 billion in sales in 2025 and that means that Spirit AeroSystems was significantly more upbeat on its revenues than analysts. Also, for the years ahead, there was no year in which Spirit’s projection came even close to analysts' expectations.

Strong Q4 Is Where All Positives Seem To End

The strong fourth quarter cash performance is where things seem to end for Spirit AeroSystems. It has a pro forma liquidity of $890 million. The sale of FMI was completed on the 13th of January, lifting the cash pile to more than $700 million. The company has projected free cash flow burn of $650 million to $700 million for H1 2025 and virtually that means that the company will lean on the remaining customer credit from Boeing and Airbus. For Spirit, it seems that it really has to walk a fine line until the acquisition by Boeing is completed later year.

Conclusion: Spirit AeroSystems Remains A Sell

I believe that Spirit AeroSystems stock remains a sell. The company saw strong performance in Q4 2024. However, the work stoppage at Boeing has a propagating effect into 2025 which leaves the company dependent on the customer credits supplied. I believe that at this time it might be prudent to sell the stock and look for other investment opportunities.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) on Friday fell about 1% for the week, underscoring a tough February overall for markets.

The enthusiasm that swept Wall Street after Donald Trump's election victory last year and inauguration last month has waned in the face of uncertainties caused by the U.S. president's tariff moves, along with data that has pointed to sticky inflation and a slowdown in the economy.

One of the biggest events this week was chip giant Nvidia's (NVDA) quarterly results. The company's blockbuster earnings over the last two years have turned it into the world's second-biggest firm by market capitalization. Despite strong fiscal fourth-quarter results, the stock fell more than 8% in the wake of massive expectations.

Tariff drama continued to dominate headlines this week as well. Trump confirmed that previously delayed 25% tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico would indeed go into effect in March, along with an additional 10% tariff on imports from China. Trump also met with Ukraine President Volodymyr Zelenskyy at the White House to discuss efforts to bring an end to the Russia-Ukraine war and sign a minerals deal. However, the meeting turned heated, and Zelenskyy was seen leaving the White House with no deal signed.

The January core personal consumption expenditures price index reading was another highlight of the week. The metric—widely seen as the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge—came in line with consensus estimates.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) slipped -1.0%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) slumped -3.5%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) bucked the trend, rising +1.0%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

Stellantis (STLA) Reports Disappointing Results: It's Time To Throw In The Towel.
